02 Clarity Compass Explained - YouTube.mp4
There are four elements that form a clarity compass and they are: One, your three-year target. Two, your company purpose. Three, your strategic anchors. And four, your core values. Here, let me show you how it works. Your three-year target serves as your North Star. This is your primary business objective that everyone in the company is driving toward. Now, I think it's important, especially in this day and age, to acknowledge that the goal of every business is to make money. I'm going to say that again because it's both important and yet oddly controversial. The goal of your business is to make money. Even if you're running a nonprofit, you still need profits to operate. Sure, you might not be taxed on those profits, but that's... That's just a tax designation, it's not a business model.
I don't care what type of operation you're running, your inflows must outpace your outflows. And ideally those inflows will increase at a faster rate than the outflows. So yes, you want to create freedom and opportunity for yourself and your team. And yes, you want to improve the lives of the people you serve. But none of that can happen unless your business turns a profit. And so that's why we encourage all entrepreneurs without apology to put their company. company's business objectives front and center. Anyone who says you're selfish for doing this is either ignorant or delusional to how business works, or they're just really, really bad at math. Okay, so we have our three-year target, our primary business goal.
But while it's vital that you have a clear plan for profitable growth, a three-year target alone is not sufficient, and that's where your company's purpose comes into play. Your company's purpose is the drive force, the why, that pushes you and your team towards your three-year targets. It's what gets you out of bed and makes it possible to persist through those really, really crappy days. Now, you may still be in the mode where you're saying, 'Our company's purpose is to simply survive, right? To pay all our bills and stay in business another day.' And look, in the early days, that's fine. Appropriate, even. But as your company scales, mere survival won't be enough to inspire your team. And it certainly won't be enough to inspire your customers.
That's why it's critical that you tap into a deeper purpose, which is exactly what I'm going to show you how to do. Okay, so we have our three-year target, and And we have our company purpose that's pushing us towards that target. But how do we make sure we don't get off track? Core values come into play. Core values are what ensure our behavior as a company and as a team stay on track because we certainly don't want to achieve our goals at the expense of our soul. So while core values ensure our internal actions and decisions stay on track, strategic anchors are what ensure our external actions and decisions stay on track. Because here's the deal. As your company scales, you will become inundated with ideas and opportunities.
That's why it's vital that you have a framework for saying yes to some projects. and no to most projects. Strategic anchors provide this framework for saying no. And when paired with core values, they keep you and everyone in your org... aligned and on track as you press onwards towards your company's goals. Said another way, the three-year target is the destination. The company purpose... is the fuel and the core values and strategic anchors are literally the guardrails that keep the vehicle that is your business between yellow lines. This is what makes the Clarity Compass such a powerful tool. It gives you and everyone in your organization clarity of direction, clarity of purpose, and clarity of action. So, now that you know what a Clarity Compass is, let's build one for your business.
Now, two quick programming notes before we dive in. First, I'm going to walk you through the Clarity Compass and the steps to building one out, but don't worry about completing it. Leading yours right now in real time. All right. I want you to understand what a clarity compass is, but the actual creation of your company's clarity compass should happen during a dedicated clarity day planning session, which will take place during your very first quarterly sprint planning meeting. So again, watch, learn, and take lots of notes, but don't feel like you need to build your clarity compass during this module. In fact, I don't want you— to build your clarity compass during this module. Instead, wait until the clarity day because collaboration is the key to achieving lasting clarity and alignment.
Second programming note: you're about to notice that there are a lot of terms that I don't use. Terms like mission, vision, and even strategy. Strategy. Reason being, in our experience, no one actually agrees on what any of these terms even mean. Now, I've heard one book use the term 'vision' to describe what another book calls 'mission' and still other books seem to confuse 'strategy' with 'goals' and, you know, even 'projects'. So to me, when no one agrees on what a word means, that word ceases to be helpful. And this is absolutely the case with terms like 'vision', 'mission', 'strategy', you get the idea. So that's why we use terms like three-year targets, company purpose, and strategic anchors. And while I know that some of this vocabulary is new, the concepts certainly are not. So hang in there. Okay, now that you know what a clarity compass is, let's walk through the steps of creating one for your business.
